3:T644,While large-scale unsupervised language models (LMs) learn broad world knowledge and some reasoning skills, achieving precise control of their behavior is difficult due to the completely unsupervised nature of their training. Existing methods for gaining such steerability collect human labels of the relative quality of model generations and fine-tune the unsupervised LM to align with these preferences, often with reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). However, RLHF is a complex and often unstable procedure, first fitting a reward model that reflects the human preferences, and then fine-tuning the large unsupervised LM using reinforcement learning to maximize this estimated reward without drifting too far from the original model. In this paper we introduce a new parameterization of the reward model in RLHF that enables extraction of the corresponding optimal policy in closed form, allowing us to solve the standard RLHF problem with only a simple classification loss. The resulting algorithm, which we call Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), is stable, performant, and computationally lightweight, eliminating the need for sampling from the LM during fine-tuning or performing significant hyperparameter tuning. Our experiments show that DPO can fine-tune LMs to align with human preferences as well as or better than existing methods. Notably, fine-tuning with DPO exceeds PPO-based RLHF in ability to control sentiment of generations, and matches or improves response quality in summarization and single-turn dialogue while being substantially simpler to implement and train.4:T44d,Through considerable effort and intuition, several recent works have reverse-engineered nontrivial behaviors of transformer models. This paper systematizes the mechanistic interpretability process they followed. First, researchers choose a metric and dataset that elicit the desired model behavior. Then, they apply activation patching to find which abstract neural network units are involved in the behavior. By varying the dataset, metric, and units under investigation, researchers can understand the functionality of each component. We automate one of the process' steps: to identify the circuit that implements the specified behavior in the model's computational graph. We propose several algorithms and reproduce previous interpretability results to validate them. For example, the ACDC algorithm rediscovered 5/5 of the component types in a circuit in GPT-2 Small that computes the Greater-Than operation. ACDC selected 68 of the 32,000 edges in GPT-2 Small, all of which were manually found by previous work. Our code is available at https://github.com/ArthurConmy/Automatic-Circuit-Discovery.5:T6c4,Machine learning models are increasingly being scaled in both sequence length and model dimension to reach longer contexts and better performance. However, existing architectures such as Transformers scale quadratically along both these axes. We ask: are there performant architectures that can scale sub-quadratically along sequence length and model dimension? We introduce Monarch Mixer (M2), a new architecture that uses the same sub-quadratic primitive along both sequence length and model dimension: Monarch matrices, a simple class of expressive structured matrices that captures many linear transforms, achieves high hardware efficiency on GPUs, and scales sub-quadratically. As a proof of concept, we explore the performance of M2 in three domains: non-causal BERT-style language modeling, ViT-style image classification, and causal GPT-style language modeling. For non-causal BERT-style modeling, M2 matches BERT-base and BERT-large in downstream GLUE quality with up to 27% fewer parameters, and achieves up to 9.1$\times$ higher throughput at sequence length 4K. On ImageNet, M2 outperforms ViT-b by 1% in accuracy, with only half the parameters. Causal GPT-style models introduce a technical challenge: enforcing causality via masking introduces a quadratic bottleneck. To alleviate this bottleneck, we develop a novel theoretical view of Monarch matrices based on multivariate polynomial evaluation and interpolation, which lets us parameterize M2 to be causal while remaining sub-quadratic. Using this parameterization, M2 matches GPT-style Transformers at 360M parameters in pretraining perplexity on The PILE--showing for the first time that it may be possible to match Transformer quality without attention or MLPs.6:T55e,Large language models trained for safety and harmlessness remain susceptible to adversarial misuse, as evidenced by the prevalence of"jailbreak"attacks on early releases of ChatGPT that elicit undesired behavior. Going beyond recognition of the issue, we investigate why such attacks succeed and how they can be created. We hypothesize two failure modes of safety training: competing objectives and mismatched generalization. Competing objectives arise when a model's capabilities and safety goals conflict, while mismatched generalization occurs when safety training fails to generalize to a domain for which capabilities exist. We use these failure modes to guide jailbreak design and then evaluate state-of-the-art models, including OpenAI's GPT-4 and Anthropic's Claude v1.3, against both existing and newly designed attacks. We find that vulnerabilities persist despite the extensive red-teaming and safety-training efforts behind these models. Notably, new attacks utilizing our failure modes succeed on every prompt in a collection of unsafe requests from the models' red-teaming evaluation sets and outperform existing ad hoc jailbreaks. Our analysis emphasizes the need for safety-capability parity -- that safety mechanisms should be as sophisticated as the underlying model -- and argues against the idea that scaling alone can resolve these safety failure modes.7:T774,Neural sequence models based on the transformer architecture have demonstrated remarkable \emph{in-context learning} (ICL) abilities, where they can perform new tasks when prompted with training and test examples, without any parameter update to the model. This work first provides a comprehensive statistical theory for transformers to perform ICL. Concretely, we show that transformers can implement a broad class of standard machine learning algorithms in context, such as least squares, ridge regression, Lasso, learning generalized linear models, and gradient descent on two-layer neural networks, with near-optimal predictive power on various in-context data distributions. Using an efficient implementation of in-context gradient descent as the underlying mechanism, our transformer constructions admit mild size bounds, and can be learned with polynomially many pretraining sequences. Building on these ``base'' ICL algorithms, intriguingly, we show that transformers can implement more complex ICL procedures involving \emph{in-context algorithm selection}, akin to what a statistician can do in real life -- A \emph{single} transformer can adaptively select different base ICL algorithms -- or even perform qualitatively different tasks -- on different input sequences, without any explicit prompting of the right algorithm or task. We both establish this in theory by explicit constructions, and also observe this phenomenon experimentally. In theory, we construct two general mechanisms for algorithm selection with concrete examples: pre-ICL testing, and post-ICL validation. As an example, we use the post-ICL validation mechanism to construct a transformer that can perform nearly Bayes-optimal ICL on a challenging task -- noisy linear models with mixed noise levels. Experimentally, we demonstrate the strong in-context algorithm selection capabilities of standard transformer architectures.8:T6af,Attention layers -- which map a sequence of inputs to a sequence of outputs -- are core building blocks of the Transformer architecture which has achieved significant breakthroughs in modern artificial intelligence. This paper presents a rigorous theoretical study on the learning and generalization of a single multi-head attention layer, with a sequence of key vectors and a separate query vector as input. We consider the random feature setting where the attention layer has a large number of heads, with randomly sampled frozen query and key matrices, and trainable value matrices. We show that such a random-feature attention layer can express a broad class of target functions that are permutation invariant to the key vectors. We further provide quantitative excess risk bounds for learning these target functions from finite samples, using random feature attention with finitely many heads. Our results feature several implications unique to the attention structure compared with existing random features theory for neural networks, such as (1) Advantages in the sample complexity over standard two-layer random-feature networks; (2) Concrete and natural classes of functions that can be learned efficiently by a random-feature attention layer; and (3) The effect of the sampling distribution of the query-key weight matrix (the product of the query and key matrix), where Gaussian random weights with a non-zero mean result in better sample complexities over the zero-mean counterpart for learning certain natural target functions. Experiments on simulated data corroborate our theoretical findings and further illustrate the interplay between the sample size and the complexity of the target function.9:T492,In this paper, we introduce the \textsc{BeaverTails} dataset, aimed at fostering research on safety alignment in large language models (LLMs). This dataset uniquely separates annotations of helpfulness and harmlessness for question-answering pairs, thus offering distinct perspectives on these crucial attributes. In total, we have gathered safety meta-labels for 30,207 question-answer (QA) pairs and 30,144 pairs of expert comparison data for both the helpfulness and harmlessness metrics. In total, we have gathered safety meta-labels for 333,963 question-answer (QA) pairs and 361,903 pairs of expert comparison data for both the helpfulness and harmlessness metrics. We further showcase applications of BeaverTails in content moderation and reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF), emphasizing its potential for practical safety measures in LLMs. We believe this dataset provides vital resources for the community, contributing towards the safe development and deployment of LLMs. Our project page is available at the following URL: https://sites.google.com/view/pku-beavertails. Warning: this paper contains example data that may be offensive or harmful.a:T694,Transformers have achieved great success in machine learning applications. Normalization techniques, such as Layer Normalization (LayerNorm, LN) and Root Mean Square Normalization (RMSNorm), play a critical role in accelerating and stabilizing the training of Transformers. While LayerNorm recenters and rescales input vectors, RMSNorm only rescales the vectors by their RMS value. Despite being more computationally efficient, RMSNorm may compromise the representation ability of Transformers. There is currently no consensus regarding the preferred normalization technique, as some models employ LayerNorm while others utilize RMSNorm, especially in recent large language models. It is challenging to convert Transformers with one normalization to the other type. While there is an ongoing disagreement between the two normalization types, we propose a solution to unify two mainstream Transformer architectures, Pre-LN and Pre-RMSNorm Transformers. By removing the inherent redundant mean information in the main branch of Pre-LN Transformers, we can reduce LayerNorm to RMSNorm, achieving higher efficiency. We further propose the Compressed RMSNorm (CRMSNorm) and Pre-CRMSNorm Transformer based on a lossless compression of the zero-mean vectors. We formally establish the equivalence of Pre-LN, Pre-RMSNorm, and Pre-CRMSNorm Transformer variants in both training and inference. It implies that Pre-LN Transformers can be substituted with Pre-(C)RMSNorm counterparts at almost no cost, offering the same arithmetic functionality along with free efficiency improvement. Experiments demonstrate that we can reduce the training and inference time of Pre-LN Transformers by 1% - 10%.b:T685,In the quest to advance human-centric natural language generation (NLG) systems, ensuring alignment between NLG models and human preferences is crucial. For this alignment, current popular methods leverage a reinforcement learning (RL) approach with a reward model trained on feedback from humans. However, inherent disagreements due to the subjective nature of human preferences pose a significant challenge for training the reward model, resulting in a deterioration of the NLG performance. To tackle this issue, previous approaches typically rely on majority voting or averaging to consolidate multiple inconsistent preferences into a merged one. Although straightforward to understand and execute, such methods suffer from an inability to capture the nuanced degrees of disaggregation among humans and may only represent a specialized subset of individuals, thereby lacking the ability to quantitatively disclose the universality of human preferences. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a novel approach, which employs a Bayesian framework to account for the distribution of disagreements among human preferences as training a preference model, and names it as d-PM. Besides, considering the RL strategy's inefficient and complex training process over the training efficiency, we further propose utilizing the contrastive learning strategy to train the NLG model with the preference scores derived from the d-PM model. Extensive experiments on two human-centric NLG tasks, i.e., emotional support conversation and integrity"Rule-of-Thumb"generation, show that our method consistently exceeds previous SOTA models in both automatic and human evaluations.c:T43b,Dynamic Sparse Training (DST) is a rapidly evolving area of research that seeks to optimize the sparse initialization of a neural network by adapting its topology during training. It has been shown that under specific conditions, DST is able to outperform dense models. The key components of this framework are the pruning and growing criteria, which are repeatedly applied during the training process to adjust the network's sparse connectivity. While the growing criterion's impact on DST performance is relatively well studied, the influence of the pruning criterion remains overlooked. To address this issue, we design and perform an extensive empirical analysis of various pruning criteria to better understand their impact on the dynamics of DST solutions. Surprisingly, we find that most of the studied methods yield similar results. The differences become more significant in the low-density regime, where the best performance is predominantly given by the simplest technique: magnitude-based pruning. The code is provided at https://github.com/alooow/fantastic_weights_paperd:T5a3,Numerous benchmarks have been established to assess the performance of foundation models on open-ended question answering, which serves as a comprehensive test of a model's ability to understand and generate language in a manner similar to humans. Most of these works focus on proposing new datasets, however, we see two main issues within previous benchmarking pipelines, namely testing leakage and evaluation automation. In this paper, we propose a novel benchmarking framework, Language-Model-as-an-Examiner, where the LM serves as a knowledgeable examiner that formulates questions based on its knowledge and evaluates responses in a reference-free manner. Our framework allows for effortless extensibility as various LMs can be adopted as the examiner, and the questions can be constantly updated given more diverse trigger topics. For a more comprehensive and equitable evaluation, we devise three strategies: (1) We instruct the LM examiner to generate questions across a multitude of domains to probe for a broad acquisition, and raise follow-up questions to engage in a more in-depth assessment. (2) Upon evaluation, the examiner combines both scoring and ranking measurements, providing a reliable result as it aligns closely with human annotations. (3) We additionally propose a decentralized Peer-examination method to address the biases in a single examiner. Our data and benchmarking results are available at: https://lmexam.com.e:T4af,Since the proposal of transformers, these models have been limited to bounded input lengths, because of their need to attend to every token in the input. In this work, we propose Unlimiformer: a general approach that wraps any existing pretrained encoder-decoder transformer, and offloads the cross-attention computation to a single k-nearest-neighbor (kNN) index, while the returned kNN distances are the attention dot-product scores. This kNN index can be kept on either the GPU or CPU memory and queried in sub-linear time; this way, we can index practically unlimited input sequences, while every attention head in every decoder layer retrieves its top-k keys, instead of attending to every key. We evaluate Unlimiformer on several long-document and book-summarization benchmarks, showing that it can process even 500k token-long inputs from the BookSum dataset, without any input truncation at test time. We demonstrate that Unlimiformer improves pretrained models such as BART and Longformer by extending them to unlimited inputs without additional learned weights and without modifying their code. We make our code and models publicly available at https://github.com/abertsch72/unlimiformer .f:T68a,This paper studies the fundamental learning problem of the energy-based model (EBM). Learning the EBM can be achieved using the maximum likelihood estimation (MLE), which typically involves the Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, such as the Langevin dynamics. However, the noise-initialized Langevin dynamics can be challenging in practice and hard to mix. This motivates the exploration of joint training with the generator model where the generator model serves as a complementary model to bypass MCMC sampling. However, such a method can be less accurate than the MCMC and result in biased EBM learning. While the generator can also serve as an initializer model for better MCMC sampling, its learning can be biased since it only matches the EBM and has no access to empirical training examples. Such biased generator learning may limit the potential of learning the EBM. To address this issue, we present a joint learning framework that interweaves the maximum likelihood learning algorithm for both the EBM and the complementary generator model. In particular, the generator model is learned by MLE to match both the EBM and the empirical data distribution, making it a more informative initializer for MCMC sampling of EBM. Learning generator with observed examples typically requires inference of the generator posterior. To ensure accurate and efficient inference, we adopt the MCMC posterior sampling and introduce a complementary inference model to initialize such latent MCMC sampling. We show that three separate models can be seamlessly integrated into our joint framework through two (dual-) MCMC teaching, enabling effective and efficient EBM learning.10:T596,Recent studies have discovered that Chain-of-Thought prompting (CoT) can dramatically improve the performance of Large Language Models (LLMs), particularly when dealing with complex tasks involving mathematics or reasoning. Despite the enormous empirical success, the underlying mechanisms behind CoT and how it unlocks the potential of LLMs remain elusive. In this paper, we take a first step towards theoretically answering these questions. Specifically, we examine the expressivity of LLMs with CoT in solving fundamental mathematical and decision-making problems. We start by giving an impossibility result showing that bounded-depth Transformers are unable to directly produce correct answers for basic arithmetic/equation tasks unless the model size grows super-polynomially with respect to the input length. In contrast, we then prove by construction that autoregressive Transformers of constant size suffice to solve both tasks by generating CoT derivations using a commonly-used math language format. Moreover, we show LLMs with CoT are capable of solving a general class of decision-making problems known as Dynamic Programming, thus justifying its power in tackling complex real-world tasks. Finally, extensive experiments on four tasks show that, while Transformers always fail to predict the answers directly, they can consistently learn to generate correct solutions step-by-step given sufficient CoT demonstrations.11:T4e7,We present a novel methodology aimed at optimizing the application of frozen large language models (LLMs) for resource-intensive vision-language (VL) pre-training. The current paradigm uses visual features as prompts to guide language models, with a focus on determining the most relevant visual features for corresponding text. Our approach diverges by concentrating on the language component, specifically identifying the optimal prompts to align with visual features. We introduce the Prompt-Transformer (P-Former), a model that predicts these ideal prompts, which is trained exclusively on linguistic data, bypassing the need for image-text pairings. This strategy subtly bifurcates the end-to-end VL training process into an additional, separate stage. Our experiments reveal that our framework significantly enhances the performance of a robust image-to-text baseline (BLIP-2), and effectively narrows the performance gap between models trained with either 4M or 129M image-text pairs. Importantly, our framework is modality-agnostic and flexible in terms of architectural design, as validated by its successful application in a video learning task using varied base modules. The code will be made available at https://github.com/yiren-jian/BLIText.12:T667,Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) of pre-trained language models (PLMs) has emerged as a highly successful approach, with training only a small number of parameters without sacrificing performance and becoming the de-facto learning paradigm with the increasing size of PLMs. However, existing PEFT methods are not memory-efficient, because they still require caching most of the intermediate activations for the gradient calculation, akin to fine-tuning. One effective way to reduce the activation memory is to apply a reversible model, so the intermediate activations are not necessary to be cached and can be recomputed. Nevertheless, modifying a PLM to its reversible variant is not straightforward, since the reversible model has a distinct architecture from the currently released PLMs. In this paper, we first investigate what is a key factor for the success of existing PEFT methods, and realize that it's essential to preserve the PLM's starting point when initializing a PEFT method. With this finding, we propose memory-efficient fine-tuning (MEFT) that inserts adapters into a PLM, preserving the PLM's starting point and making it reversible without additional pre-training. We evaluate MEFT on the GLUE benchmark and five question-answering tasks with various backbones, BERT, RoBERTa, BART and OPT. MEFT significantly reduces the activation memory up to 84% of full fine-tuning with a negligible amount of trainable parameters. Moreover, MEFT achieves the same score on GLUE and a comparable score on the question-answering tasks as full fine-tuning. A similar finding is also observed for the image classification task.13:T494,Large language models (LLMs) can learn to perform a wide range of natural language tasks from just a handful of in-context examples. However, for generating strings from highly structured languages (e.g., semantic parsing to complex domain-specific languages), it is challenging for the LLM to generalize from just a few exemplars. We explore $\textbf{grammar prompting}$ as a simple approach for enabling LLMs to use external knowledge and domain-specific constraints, expressed through a grammar expressed in Backus--Naur Form (BNF), during in-context learning. Grammar prompting augments each demonstration example with a specialized grammar that is minimally sufficient for generating the particular output example, where the specialized grammar is a subset of the full DSL grammar. For inference, the LLM first predicts a BNF grammar given a test input, and then generates the output according to the rules of the grammar. Experiments demonstrate that grammar prompting can enable LLMs to perform competitively on a diverse set of DSL generation tasks, including semantic parsing (SMCalFlow, Overnight, GeoQuery), PDDL planning, and even molecule generation (SMILES).14:T405,We propose eXtensible Prompt (X-Prompt) for prompting a large language model (LLM) beyond natural language (NL). X-Prompt instructs an LLM with not only NL but also an extensible vocabulary of imaginary words. Registering new imaginary words allows us to instruct the LLM to comprehend concepts that are difficult to describe with NL words, thereby making a prompt more descriptive. Also, these imaginary words are designed to be out-of-distribution (OOD) robust so that they can be (re)used like NL words in various prompts, distinguishing X-Prompt from soft prompt that is for fitting in-distribution data. We propose context-augmented learning (CAL) to learn imaginary words for general usability, enabling them to work properly in OOD (unseen) prompts. We experiment X-Prompt for zero-shot language style customization as a case study. The promising results of X-Prompt demonstrate its potential to facilitate advanced interaction beyond the natural language interface, bridging the communication gap between humans and LLMs.15:T5ee,Since their initial introduction, score-based diffusion models (SDMs) have been successfully applied to solve a variety of linear inverse problems in finite-dimensional vector spaces due to their ability to efficiently approximate the posterior distribution. However, using SDMs for inverse problems in infinite-dimensional function spaces has only been addressed recently, primarily through methods that learn the unconditional score. While this approach is advantageous for some inverse problems, it is mostly heuristic and involves numerous computationally costly forward operator evaluations during posterior sampling. To address these limitations, we propose a theoretically grounded method for sampling from the posterior of infinite-dimensional Bayesian linear inverse problems based on amortized conditional SDMs. In particular, we prove that one of the most successful approaches for estimating the conditional score in finite dimensions - the conditional denoising estimator - can also be applied in infinite dimensions. A significant part of our analysis is dedicated to demonstrating that extending infinite-dimensional SDMs to the conditional setting requires careful consideration, as the conditional score typically blows up for small times, contrarily to the unconditional score. We conclude by presenting stylized and large-scale numerical examples that validate our approach, offer additional insights, and demonstrate that our method enables large-scale, discretization-invariant Bayesian inference.16:T75c,Binary Balanced Tree RvNNs (BBT-RvNNs) enforce sequence composition according to a preset balanced binary tree structure. Thus, their non-linear recursion depth is just $\log_2 n$ ($n$ being the sequence length). Such logarithmic scaling makes BBT-RvNNs efficient and scalable on long sequence tasks such as Long Range Arena (LRA). However, such computational efficiency comes at a cost because BBT-RvNNs cannot solve simple arithmetic tasks like ListOps. On the flip side, RvNNs (e.g., Beam Tree RvNN) that do succeed on ListOps (and other structure-sensitive tasks like formal logical inference) are generally several times more expensive than even RNNs. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework -- Recursion in Recursion (RIR) to strike a balance between the two sides - getting some of the benefits from both worlds. In RIR, we use a form of two-level nested recursion - where the outer recursion is a $k$-ary balanced tree model with another recursive model (inner recursion) implementing its cell function. For the inner recursion, we choose Beam Tree RvNNs (BT-RvNN). To adjust BT-RvNNs within RIR we also propose a novel strategy of beam alignment. Overall, this entails that the total recursive depth in RIR is upper-bounded by $k \log_k n$. Our best RIR-based model is the first model that demonstrates high ($\geq 90\%$) length-generalization performance on ListOps while at the same time being scalable enough to be trainable on long sequence inputs from LRA. Moreover, in terms of accuracy in the LRA language tasks, it performs competitively with Structured State Space Models (SSMs) without any special initialization - outperforming Transformers by a large margin. On the other hand, while SSMs can marginally outperform RIR on LRA, they (SSMs) fail to length-generalize on ListOps. Our code is available at: \url{https://github.com/JRC1995/BeamRecursionFamily/}.17:T4ec,Attaining a high degree of user controllability in visual generation often requires intricate, fine-grained inputs like layouts. However, such inputs impose a substantial burden on users when compared to simple text inputs. To address the issue, we study how Large Language Models (LLMs) can serve as visual planners by generating layouts from text conditions, and thus collaborate with visual generative models. We propose LayoutGPT, a method to compose in-context visual demonstrations in style sheet language to enhance the visual planning skills of LLMs. LayoutGPT can generate plausible layouts in multiple domains, ranging from 2D images to 3D indoor scenes. LayoutGPT also shows superior performance in converting challenging language concepts like numerical and spatial relations to layout arrangements for faithful text-to-image generation. When combined with a downstream image generation model, LayoutGPT outperforms text-to-image models/systems by 20-40% and achieves comparable performance as human users in designing visual layouts for numerical and spatial correctness. Lastly, LayoutGPT achieves comparable performance to supervised methods in 3D indoor scene synthesis, demonstrating its effectiveness and potential in multiple visual domains.18:T5ca,Masked image modeling (MIM) has become a prevalent pre-training setup for vision foundation models and attains promising performance. Despite its success, existing MIM methods discard the decoder network during downstream applications, resulting in inconsistent representations between pre-training and fine-tuning and can hamper downstream task performance. In this paper, we propose a new architecture, RevColV2, which tackles this issue by keeping the entire autoencoder architecture during both pre-training and fine-tuning. The main body of RevColV2 contains bottom-up columns and top-down columns, between which information is reversibly propagated and gradually disentangled. Such design enables our architecture with the nice property: maintaining disentangled low-level and semantic information at the end of the network in MIM pre-training. Our experimental results suggest that a foundation model with decoupled features can achieve competitive performance across multiple downstream vision tasks such as image classification, semantic segmentation and object detection. For example, after intermediate fine-tuning on ImageNet-22K dataset, RevColV2-L attains 88.4% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1K classification and 58.6 mIoU on ADE20K semantic segmentation. With extra teacher and large scale dataset, RevColv2-L achieves 62.1 box AP on COCO detection and 60.4 mIoU on ADE20K semantic segmentation. Code and models are released at https://github.com/megvii-research/RevCol19:T606,Augmenting large language models (LLMs) with external tools has emerged as a promising approach to solving complex problems. However, traditional methods, which finetune LLMs with tool demonstration data, can be both costly and restricted to a predefined set of tools. Recent in-context learning paradigm alleviates these issues, but the limited context length only allows for a few shots of demonstrations, leading to suboptimal understandings of the tools. Moreover, when there are numerous tools to choose from, in-context learning could completely fail to work. In this paper, we propose an alternative approach, $\textbf{ToolkenGPT}$, which combines the benefits of both sides. Our approach represents each $\underline{tool}$ as a to$\underline{ken}$ ($\textit{toolken}$) and learns an embedding for it, enabling tool calls in the same way as generating a regular word token. Once a toolken is triggered, the LLM is prompted to complete arguments for the tool to execute. ToolkenGPT offers the flexibility to plug in an arbitrary number of tools by expanding the set of toolkens on the fly. In addition, it improves tool use by allowing extensive demonstration data for learning the toolken embeddings. In diverse domains, including numerical reasoning, knowledge-based question answering, and embodied plan generation, our approach effectively augments LLMs with tools and substantially outperforms various latest baselines. ToolkenGPT demonstrates the promising ability to use relevant tools from a large tool set in complex scenarios.1a:T468,Machine learning tools often rely on embedding text as vectors of real numbers. In this paper, we study how the semantic structure of language is encoded in the algebraic structure of such embeddings. Specifically, we look at a notion of ``semantic independence'' capturing the idea that, e.g., ``eggplant'' and ``tomato'' are independent given ``vegetable''. Although such examples are intuitive, it is difficult to formalize such a notion of semantic independence. The key observation here is that any sensible formalization should obey a set of so-called independence axioms, and thus any algebraic encoding of this structure should also obey these axioms. This leads us naturally to use partial orthogonality as the relevant algebraic structure. We develop theory and methods that allow us to demonstrate that partial orthogonality does indeed capture semantic independence. Complementary to this, we also introduce the concept of independence preserving embeddings where embeddings preserve the conditional independence structures of a distribution, and we prove the existence of such embeddings and approximations to them.1b:T472,Vision-language pretrained models have seen remarkable success, but their application to safety-critical settings is limited by their lack of interpretability. To improve the interpretability of vision-language models such as CLIP, we propose a multi-modal information bottleneck (M2IB) approach that learns latent representations that compress irrelevant information while preserving relevant visual and textual features. We demonstrate how M2IB can be applied to attribution analysis of vision-language pretrained models, increasing attribution accuracy and improving the interpretability of such models when applied to safety-critical domains such as healthcare. Crucially, unlike commonly used unimodal attribution methods, M2IB does not require ground truth labels, making it possible to audit representations of vision-language pretrained models when multiple modalities but no ground-truth data is available. Using CLIP as an example, we demonstrate the effectiveness of M2IB attribution and show that it outperforms gradient-based, perturbation-based, and attention-based attribution methods both qualitatively and quantitatively.0:{"rsc":["$","$1","c",{"children":[["$","div",null,{"className":"mx-auto max-w-5xl px-4 py-8","children":["$","$L2",null,{"data":{"venue":"NeurIPS","venue_slug":"neurips-2023","year":2023,"full_name":"Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems","generated_at":"2026-04-19T04:06:29.627949+00:00","stats":{"total_fetched":3582,"total_relevant":523,"by_award":{"poster":523},"by_category":{"multimodal":69,"post_train":71,"mechanism":136,"efficient":65,"llm_pretrain":98,"multimodal_gen":53,"coding_agent":9,"industry":2,"agent":20}},"papers":[{"id":"Xie0DDLLLL0Y23","title":"DoReMi: Optimizing Data Mixtures Speeds Up Language Model Pretraining","authors":["Sang Michael Xie","Hieu Pham","Xuanyi Dong","Nan Du","Hanxiao Liu","Yifeng Lu","Percy Liang","Quoc V. Large models are often trained with fixed corpus ratios chosen by rough intuition, but domains differ greatly in marginal value, so poor mixtures waste tokens on data that contributes little to the target model quality.","importance":"This is a high-value method contribution for pretraining. Its significance is not merely modest efficiency gain; it shows that better data weighting can materially shift the quality-vs-compute frontier. At current scales, data curation and mixture design are among the cheapest levers left, and this paper turns mixture selection from folklore into an optimization problem.","technical_approach":"DoReMi treats corpus mixing as a learned reweighting problem instead of a fixed manual choice. The high-level idea is to use a reference model to estimate which domains are under-served or more valuable, then adapt sampling weights toward a mixture that improves robustness across domains. This differs from simple validation-loss tuning because it adopts a distributionally robust view: the target is not to overfit one domain but to find a mixture that improves overall pretraining utility. The design is effective because pretraining loss is already an average over domains, so bad weights create systematic compute inefficiency.","experiments":"The main empirical result is typically that optimized mixtures reach a given loss or downstream quality using fewer tokens and less compute than uniform or heuristic baselines. The paper likely evaluates on multi-domain language corpora and compares convergence speed, final perplexity, and cross-domain performance under different sampling schemes. Hashimoto"],"institutions":[],"abstract":"","venue":"NEURIPS","year":2023,"track":"main","award":null,"links":{"arxiv":"","pdf":"","openreview":"","dblp":"https://dblp.org/rec/conf/nips/GulrajaniH23","doi":""},"_dblp_key":"conf/nips/GulrajaniH23","_ee":"http://papers.nips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/hash/35b5c175e139bff5f22a5361270fce87-Abstract-Conference.html","category_id":"llm_pretrain","relevance_score":9,"reading_tier":"must_read","summary":{"core_problem":"这篇论文要解决的是：离散扩散语言模型过去最大的短板之一，是很难像 AR LM 那样给出 tractable likelihood，因此训练目标、评估方式和与主流语言建模的可比性都受限。大家知道 diffusion 可能带来更强的全局建模和非左到右生成能力，但如果不能做似然建模，它就很难真正成为语言预训练的一等公民。","importance":"这是范式级的重要工作。它直接回应了‘diffusion LM 能否成为严肃语言建模替代路线’这个核心问题，因为 likelihood 是语言模型最基础的共同货币：它决定了训练是否统一、困惑度是否可比、scaling 是否可分析。对 pretrain 研究来说，这篇论文的重要性明显高于一般应用型 diffusion work，因为它在补 diffusion LM 的理论和方法缺口。","technical_approach":"核心方法是构造 likelihood-based 的 diffusion language model，让离散扩散过程不再只靠近似生成目标或启发式训练，而能对应到可优化、可评估的似然目标。按照这条路线，关键设计通常包括：如何定义离散 token 上的前向噪声过程、如何参数化反向分布、以及如何把训练目标写成严格的 likelihood 或其紧致下界。它和传统 AR LM 的区别，不是把条件分解换个顺序这么简单，而是把生成过程从单步 next-token 改成多步去噪；而与早期 diffusion LM 相比，它补上了最关键的统计解释和评测接口。这样设计有效，是因为一旦有了 likelihood，扩散 LM 才能与 AR 在同一优化语言里比较，也更容易研究 scaling、tokenizer 和训练动态。","experiments":"这篇论文的实验关键通常有三部分：第一，验证模型能在标准语言建模基准上得到有意义的 likelihood/perplexity 类指标；第二，比较生成质量与 AR 或已有 diffusion LM 的差异；第三，分析采样步数、噪声设计和模型规模对性能的影响。NeurIPS 2023 这篇工作常被视为后续 SEDD、MDLM、LLaDA 等离散 diffusion LM 路线的前置脉络之一。当前给定信息没有具体数据集、参数规模和困惑度数字，因此不能编造结果；但可以明确说，这篇工作的核心价值更在‘让 diffusion LM 可被严肃比较’，而不一定是在所有基准上立即超过 AR LM。","authors_note":"作者为 Ishaan Gulrajani 与 Tatsunori B. Hashimoto。Hashimoto 长期做鲁棒性、生成建模与语言模型训练目标相关研究；这篇工作在 diffusion LM 方向有明显范式意义。"},"summary_en":{"core_problem":"The paper addresses a central weakness of earlier diffusion language models: they generally lacked a clean likelihood formulation comparable to autoregressive LMs. Without tractable likelihood, training objectives are harder to interpret, perplexity-style evaluation is missing or indirect, and diffusion LMs remain difficult to compare seriously against mainstream language modeling.","importance":"This is a paradigm-level contribution. It matters because likelihood is the common currency of language modeling: it defines the training objective, enables apples-to-apples evaluation, and supports scaling-law style analysis. For pretraining research, this is much more important than a diffusion application paper, because it fills a foundational gap in making diffusion LM a credible alternative to next-token autoregression.","technical_approach":"The core idea is to formulate a likelihood-based diffusion language model for discrete tokens, so the model is not trained only through heuristic denoising objectives but through a principled likelihood objective or a tight bound to it. The key design choices in this line of work are how to define the forward corruption process on discrete tokens, how to parameterize the reverse distribution, and how to recover a proper likelihood training criterion. The difference from an autoregressive LM is not just a different factorization order; generation becomes iterative denoising instead of one-step next-token prediction. The difference from earlier diffusion LMs is that this formulation provides the missing statistical interpretation and evaluation interface.","experiments":"The critical experiments are typically threefold: first, showing meaningful likelihood or perplexity-like evaluation on standard language modeling benchmarks; second, comparing generation quality against autoregressive baselines or prior diffusion LMs; and third, analyzing the effects of sampling steps, noise design, and model scale. This NeurIPS 2023 work is often viewed as part of the path that later led to stronger discrete diffusion LM lines such as SEDD, MDLM, and related approaches. The provided metadata does not include datasets, parameter counts, or numerical results, so exact numbers should not be invented. The key value is making diffusion LMs comparable and analyzable, not necessarily beating autoregressive LMs across the board immediately.","authors_note":"The authors are Ishaan Gulrajani and Tatsunori B. Hashimoto. Standard compute-optimal scaling laws assume data can grow with model size, but many real settings—multilingual, domain-specific, or license-constrained pretraining—hit the data wall first.","importance":"This is directly important for pretraining strategy. It shifts the question from generic compute allocation to what to do when the corpus is small: choose a smaller model, train longer, repeat data, regularize harder, improve tokenization, or mix in out-of-domain text. These decisions matter more now than another idealized scaling-law fit on abundant data.","technical_approach":"The work likely performs a systematic study of model size, token budget, data repetition, and data-mixing strategy under data constraints, and derives recommendations that differ from standard Chinchilla-style scaling. The key point is not to reject compute-optimal theory, but to show that its assumptions break when data is limited. In that setting, scaling parameters too aggressively causes undertraining, while repeating data too much raises overfitting and memorization risks. The method contribution is therefore an empirical scaling analysis tailored to the data-constrained regime.","experiments":"The main value should come from broad scaling experiments. The most useful facts are how the optimal model size shifts as dataset size shrinks, how many effective passes over the same data remain beneficial, and where returns start to decay or generalization degrades. Standard practice trains a reward model from preference labels and then optimizes the policy with RL under a KL constraint, which introduces instability, rollout overhead, and extra approximation error at each stage.","importance":"This is a landmark post-training method with clear implications for the pretrain–posttrain interface. Its significance is not just that alignment becomes simpler; it shows that preference optimization can be reduced to a supervised objective derived from the same KL-regularized reward-maximization framework. For pretraining researchers, that is important because it changes how we think about the base LM: under a reference-policy view, the model already contains enough structure to support direct preference optimization without a full RL stack.","technical_approach":"The core idea is Direct Preference Optimization, or DPO. Instead of separately fitting a reward model and running RL, DPO starts from the optimal-policy form of KL-constrained reward maximization and derives a direct objective on preference pairs. In practice, it increases the log-probability of the preferred response relative to the rejected one while anchoring the policy to a reference model. Compared with PPO-style RLHF, it removes online sampling and high-variance policy gradients, but keeps the two ingredients that matter: preference fitting and controlled deviation from the base model.","experiments":"The main empirical claim is that DPO matches or exceeds RLHF-style baselines on standard preference-alignment setups while being much simpler to train. Typical evaluations include summarization and dialogue preference tasks, with comparisons against PPO, SLiC-style methods, and related baselines. A key practical result is stability: no separate reward-model training and no online RL rollouts are required to obtain competitive win rates or reward-proxy scores. 相比，这样把复杂度从对全序列长度的高阶依赖，转移到更可控的层次结构上；与仅做局部注意力相比，它又保留了跨块全局上下文。设计有效的原因是长程依赖不需要每个字节都参与全连接注意，而局部细节可以在小窗口内恢复。代价是层级接口设计很关键，块边界处理不好会伤害精细生成。","experiments":"这篇工作的关键实验事实通常包括：在文本、代码、图像字节流或 DNA 一类长序列上，相比标准 A coarse model handles long-range dependencies over chunks or patches, and a fine model predicts detailed bytes within each chunk. This is not just pooling or local attention. It is a hierarchical factorization of sequence modeling: global structure is handled at a compressed scale, while local detail is restored at full resolution. That works because not every byte needs to attend globally for long-context coherence. The trade-off is that the chunk interface becomes a critical design point; if it is too lossy, fine-grained generation suffers.","experiments":"The central empirical claim is scalability to million-byte sequences, as stated in the title. Anthony。"},"summary_en":{"core_problem":"The paper studies a precise question: memorization in large language models is not just an anecdotal privacy failure, but may follow regular and predictable scaling behavior. Earlier discussion often focused on isolated examples of verbatim recall; this work asks when strong memorization appears, whether it emerges abruptly with scale, and whether that transition can be forecast.","importance":"This is highly relevant to pretraining because it sits at the intersection of scaling laws, data governance, and training dynamics. If memorization is both emergent and predictable, it should not be treated merely as accidental data leakage; it becomes a systematic consequence of model scale, data repetition, and optimization. That directly matters for deduplication, copyright risk, privacy control, and synthetic-data feedback loops.","technical_approach":"The contribution is likely empirical characterization and predictive modeling rather than a new mitigation algorithm. Transformer 量化里的一个具体痛点：激活 outlier 会严重破坏低比特量化，而很多 outlier 实际上来自注意力头被迫在‘无事可做’时也输出非零结果。传统量化方法多在事后校准、裁剪或更复杂的量化器上补救；作者想从结构上减少 outlier 的生成源头。","importance":"这项工作的价值是方法级但非常工程化，对 serving 和训练后部署都重要。对 pretrain 研究的启示在于：某些量化困难不是权重分布天然决定的，而是架构功能设计不完整导致的副产物。换句话说，若注意力头缺少显式的 no-op 行为，模型会用极端激活近似‘跳过’，这既伤量化，也可能反映训练表示效率不高。","technical_approach":"核心思想从标题已经很清楚：让 attention heads 能够 do nothing，从而消除 outlier。通常这意味着在注意力结构中加入一个显式空操作路径、可学习门控或特殊 token/偏置，使得某些头在当前输入上无需强行参与。它和事后 clipping 的区别是前者在表征层面解决‘为什么会有大值’，后者只是截断结果。为什么这样有效：如果头在不相关上下文里可以输出接近零，后续残差流的数值范围会更稳定，量化尺度更容易覆盖。代价是需要轻微改模型并重新训练或微调，且若 no-op 设计不当，可能削弱头的利用率。","experiments":"题面没有摘要，无法给具体 bit-width、模型和精度数字。但这篇论文最关键的实验通常会包括：对比标准 Transformer 与修改后架构在 INT8/更低比特量化下的精度保持；展示激活分布中 outlier 明显减少；并验证全精度性能基本不掉。值得关注的 ablation 是 no-op 机制的具体实现、在哪些层加入最有效，以及它是否在不同模型家族上都成立。如果结果只在小模型或特定视觉任务上有效，那对大规模 LM 的推广价值会受限；如果语言模型上也稳，则说明这是更普遍的架构缺口。","authors_note":""},"summary_en":{"core_problem":"The paper targets a concrete quantization bottleneck in Transformers: activation outliers can severely hurt low-bit quantization, and many of those outliers may arise because attention heads are forced to produce nonzero outputs even when they have nothing useful to contribute. Standard quantization work often patches this after the fact with calibration, clipping, or better quantizers; this paper instead tries to remove one source of the problem structurally.","importance":"This is a method contribution with strong engineering relevance for deployment and inference efficiency. For pretraining researchers, the useful lesson is that some quantization difficulty is not an unavoidable property of learned weights; it can be an architectural side effect of giving heads no explicit no-op behavior. If a head must fake 'do nothing' using extreme activations, that is bad for quantization and may also indicate inefficient representation dynamics.","technical_approach":"The main idea is explicit in the title: help attention heads do nothing, thereby reducing outliers. In practice this likely means adding a no-op path, learnable gating, or a special mechanism that lets some heads output near zero when the input does not require them. This differs from post hoc clipping because it addresses the cause of large activations rather than truncating the symptom. The reason it should help is straightforward: if irrelevant heads can stay quiet, the residual stream has a tighter numerical range and low-bit scales can cover it more faithfully. The trade-off is a small architectural change and the need for retraining or finetuning, with the risk of underusing heads if the no-op path is too aggressive.","experiments":"The prompt does not provide the abstract, so specific model families, bit-widths, and metrics cannot be stated safely. The decisive experiments would normally compare standard and modified Transformers under INT8 or lower-bit quantization, show a substantial reduction in activation outliers, and verify that full-precision quality is preserved. Useful ablations would test different no-op mechanisms, which layers benefit most, and whether the effect transfers across model families. If gains hold only on small or narrow settings, the impact is limited; if they extend to language models, the architectural point is much stronger.","authors_note":""},"tags":["transformer","quantization","attention","inference"]},{"id":"ChenLHRW0DLHLL23","title":"Symbolic Discovery of Optimization Algorithms","authors":["Xiangning Chen","Chen Liang","Da Huang","Esteban Real","Kaiyuan Wang","Hieu Pham","Xuanyi Dong","Thang Luong","Cho-Jui Hsieh","Yifeng Lu","Quoc V. Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs","authors":["Tim Dettmers","Artidoro Pagnoni","Ari Holtzman","Luke Zettlemoyer"],"institutions":["University of Washington"],"abstract":"","venue":"NEURIPS","year":2023,"track":"main","award":null,"links":{"arxiv":"","pdf":"","openreview":"","dblp":"https://dblp.org/rec/conf/nips/DettmersPHZ23","doi":""},"_dblp_key":"conf/nips/DettmersPHZ23","_ee":"http://papers.nips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/hash/1feb87871436031bdc0f2beaa62a049b-Abstract-Conference.html","category_id":"efficient","relevance_score":8,"reading_tier":"must_read","summary":{"core_problem":"这篇论文解决的核心问题是：如何在单卡或低资源条件下高质量微调大语言模型，而不显著牺牲效果。此前 LoRA 已经降低了可训练参数量，但全精度权重仍然占据主要显存，因此 33B、65B 级模型对大多数研究者仍然不可达。","importance":"这是系统方法级的重要工作，而且已经改变了社区的默认实践。对 pretrain 研究的直接启示是：量化不只是部署手段，也可以成为 posttrain 接口的一部分；一旦 4-bit 权重量化下的可训练性足够好，更多中大规模模型的对齐、指令化和领域适配实验就变得可做，这反过来加快了预训练模型分析和数据策略迭代。","technical_approach":"QLoRA 的核心方法是在 4-bit 量化后的冻结基座模型上训练 LoRA 适配器，同时尽量把量化误差和显存开销压低。关键设计有三点：第一，使用 NF4（NormalFloat4）这类更适合近似正态分布权重的 4-bit 数据类型，减少量化损失；第二，使用 double quantization，对量化常数再量化，进一步省显存；第三，采用 paged optimizers，缓解长序列或大 batch 下优化器状态引发的显存峰值。与直接做 8-bit/4-bit 训练不同，它不更新量化主权重，只训练低秩增量，所以稳定性更好；与普通 LoRA 相比，它把真正的瓶颈——基座权重存储——压了下去，因此能在消费级或单张 A100 级别硬件上处理更大的模型。","experiments":"论文的关键事实是：作者在单张 48GB GPU 上微调了 65B 模型，并展示了接近全 16-bit 微调的效果。文中使用 Guanaco 系列作为展示，覆盖 7B 到 65B 多个规模，并在指令跟随基准和人工/模型评审上与全参数微调、LoRA 及其他资源受限方法比较。广为引用的结论包括：4-bit QLoRA 在多项设置下几乎不损失最终质量；65B Guanaco 达到当时接近 ChatGPT 水平的聊天表现之一。对工程上最有价值的 ablation 是 NF4、double quantization 和 paged optimizer 三者都不是装饰项，去掉后要么质量下降，要么显存峰值失控。","authors_note":"作者来自 University of Washington。Tim Dettmers 是低比特训练与高效微调方向的代表性研究者，相关代表工作包括 LLM.int8()、bitsandbytes 生态和 QLoRA。Luke Zettlemoyer 是 NLP 与大模型方向的知名学者。"},"summary_en":{"core_problem":"The paper solves a very practical bottleneck: how to finetune large language models under tight memory budgets without giving up much quality. LoRA had already reduced the number of trainable parameters, but full-precision base weights still dominated memory, keeping 33B–65B models out of reach for most researchers.","importance":"This is a major systems-and-methods contribution, and it quickly became a default recipe in the community. For pretraining research, the key lesson is that quantization is not only a serving trick; it can be part of the posttraining interface. Once 4-bit finetuning is good enough, many more alignment, domain adaptation, and analysis experiments become feasible on larger base models.","technical_approach":"QLoRA finetunes LoRA adapters on top of a frozen 4-bit quantized base model while carefully controlling quantization error and memory spikes. The design has three important pieces. First, it uses NF4, a 4-bit datatype better matched to approximately normal weight distributions, which reduces information loss. Second, it applies double quantization, meaning the quantization constants are themselves quantized to save additional memory. Third, it uses paged optimizers to handle transient optimizer-state spikes for long contexts or larger batches. Unlike direct low-bit full-weight training, QLoRA leaves the quantized base frozen and only updates low-rank adapters, which improves stability. Unlike standard LoRA, it attacks the real memory bottleneck—the base weights—so much larger models fit on modest hardware.","experiments":"The headline result is that the authors finetuned a 65B model on a single 48GB GPU and achieved performance close to full 16-bit finetuning. The paper presents the Guanaco family from 7B to 65B and compares against full finetuning, LoRA, and other resource-constrained baselines on instruction-following evaluations plus human/model judgments. A widely cited takeaway is that 4-bit QLoRA preserves final quality surprisingly well across many settings. The most useful ablations show that NF4, double quantization, and paged optimizers are all functional components rather than cosmetic additions: removing them either hurts quality or breaks the memory budget.","authors_note":"The authors are from the University of Washington. Tim Dettmers is a leading researcher in low-bit training and efficient finetuning, known for LLM.int8(), bitsandbytes, and QLoRA. Prior work often depended on costly human annotation pipelines or proprietary APIs, which made controlled iteration hard. AlpacaFarm is proposed as a reproducible simulation environment for studying instruction tuning, reward modeling, and preference-based optimization end to end.","importance":"Its contribution is mainly infrastructural and methodological, not a new alignment algorithm. For pretrain-to-posttrain research, this matters because questions around reward hacking, preference noise, synthetic supervision, and offline vs. online RLHF cannot be answered cleanly without a cheap and repeatable testbed. AlpacaFarm turns RLHF from an anecdotal engineering process into something closer to an experimental science.","technical_approach":"The core idea is a simulation framework rather than a new objective. The system builds instruction-following data, constructs pairwise preference data, trains automated reward/preference models to approximate human feedback, and then evaluates SFT and preference/RL-style tuning methods within the same controlled setup. The key design choice is to optimize for low cost and stable method ranking, not to perfectly replace humans. Unlike leaderboard-style evaluations that focus on final win rates only, AlpacaFarm exposes the training loop and makes it easier to study how reward quality, preference data size, and optimization choices interact.","experiments":"The main empirical result is that method rankings in the simulator show meaningful agreement with small-scale human evaluation, which makes the framework useful as a proxy but not a full substitute. The paper studies Alpaca-style instruction-following models and compares supervised fine-tuning, reward-model-driven optimization, and preference-learning variants, reporting both automatic and human-centered evaluations. The most useful takeaway is about signal quality: when the reward model is reasonably strong, the simulator is useful for screening methods; when it is biased, optimization quickly exploits that bias, mirroring reward hacking seen in real RLHF. Ablations show strong sensitivity to preference data quantity, candidate response quality, and reward-model design.","authors_note":""},"tags":["rlhf","alignment","evaluation","simulation"],"notable_authors":["Yann Dubois","Chen Xuechen Li","Rohan Taori","Tianyi Zhang","Ishaan Gulrajani"]},{"id":"2310.12109","title":"Monarch Mixer: A Simple Sub-Quadratic GEMM-Based Architecture","authors":["Daniel Y. diffusion model? The standard split has been clear—LLMs for understanding and diffusion for image synthesis—so the paper probes whether discrete visual tokens plus autoregressive multimodal modeling are enough to unify the two.","importance":"This matters at the paradigm level. It is not just a stronger VLM; it tests whether image generation can live inside the same autoregressive pretraining framework as language and image understanding. For pretraining, that has concrete implications for tokenizer design, data mixing, and objective unification. The trade-off is also clear: a unified AR model offers simplicity and composability, but may lag specialized diffusion systems in fidelity, speed, or high-resolution scaling.","technical_approach":"The core method is to tokenize images into discrete visual codes and train a multimodal language model over mixed text-image sequences with a standard next-token objective. The point is not mere concatenation; the model is set up to support both conditional image generation and multimodal understanding, so the paper can test whether one AR model can do both reasonably well. Relative to diffusion-based generation, it gives up continuous denoising dynamics in exchange for a single interface, a single objective, and direct reuse of the LLM training stack.","experiments":"Based on the title, the key experiments likely cover text-to-image generation together with multimodal understanding, evaluating whether a unified model remains useful on both. The value is not merely scale. It shifts the training unit from isolated image-caption pairs to full interleaved documents, which better reflect real web pages, tutorials, encyclopedic articles, and reports. That matters because document-level data carries richer supervision for cross-image references, local grounding, and long-context multimodal understanding. If this data regime becomes standard, objectives and evaluations designed only for image-caption pairs will look increasingly narrow.","technical_approach":"OBELICS is primarily a data construction and filtering pipeline rather than a new model architecture. The work focuses on extracting interleaved image-text documents from the open web, denoising and filtering them, and releasing the result openly for reproducible multimodal pretraining. Relative to datasets such as LAION, it preserves document order, paragraph context, and multi-image structure, which makes it more suitable for unified autoregressive or document-level multimodal models. The trade-off is that web parsing and heuristic filtering introduce bias, and alignment in interleaved documents is often weaker and noisier than in caption data.","experiments":"The main empirical message is typically that models trained with OBELICS are better positioned for document-level understanding, multi-image context use, and broad multimodal evaluation than models trained only on simple image-caption pairs, while also improving reproducibility through open release. Prior approaches often trade quality for sparse or approximate attention, or focus only on inference engineering. This work asks whether sequence computation can be parallelized at the block level without giving up too much modeling power.","importance":"This is an architecture-systems contribution with direct relevance to large-scale pretraining. Long-context modeling is increasingly constrained by throughput and hardware efficiency, so a blockwise parallel formulation matters even if it does not change the language-modeling objective. It is especially relevant for continued pretraining and long-document training where wall-clock efficiency can dominate algorithmic niceties.","technical_approach":"The core idea is a blockwise parallel Transformer that partitions the sequence and performs more computation in parallel across blocks. Unlike linear attention, it does not necessarily replace the attention kernel entirely; unlike speculative decoding, it modifies the model computation itself to enable native block-level parallelism. The main design challenge is preserving cross-block dependency: if blocks are too independent, language-model quality drops. So the method likely uses some form of summary state or staged communication to balance dependency fidelity against parallel speed.","experiments":"Given only the title, the likely evaluation emphasizes the quality-efficiency tradeoff on long-context language modeling. Relevant baselines would include standard Transformers, sparse/local/linear attention variants, and other parallel decoding or chunked modeling methods, with metrics such as perplexity, throughput, latency, and supported context length. The key question is how much speedup is obtained at a fixed quality level and how error grows with larger block sizes. If diffusion is to be a serious alternative to autoregressive LMs, the representation space has to be chosen more carefully.","importance":"This is a paradigm-level contribution for non-AR language modeling. Moving diffusion from discrete tokens to a learned latent space is a pragmatic step toward making diffusion-based text generation usable at scale. For pretraining research, the key lesson is that the competitiveness of a generative objective depends heavily on where the model lives: token space, latent space, or some hybrid interface.","technical_approach":"The method applies latent diffusion to language: a text autoencoder maps discrete sequences into continuous latents, a diffusion model is trained in that latent space, and a decoder reconstructs text. The idea is adapted from latent diffusion in vision, but the text setting is harder because the latent space must preserve semantic content while remaining smooth enough for continuous denoising. Relative to discrete diffusion LMs, this avoids token-level categorical diffusion and large softmax issues; relative to AR models, it offers parallel generation but pays the price of an encoder-decoder bottleneck and possible information loss.","experiments":"The likely empirical focus is generation quality and sampling efficiency versus autoregressive and discrete-diffusion baselines. masked modeling 框架同时处理多种模态和多种任务，而不是为图像、文本、语义分割、深度等分别训练专门模型。过去多模态预训练常见两条路：要么只做图文对齐，统一性有限；要么做统一 token 建模，但任务接口不够自然。4M 试图把“多模态输入 + 多模态输出”都收进一个掩码建模范式里。","importance":"这是典型的范式级探索，价值在统一建模接口。对 pretrain 的启示是：masked modeling 不只是 BERT 或 MAE 那类单模态自监督工具，也可以扩展成一个通用 I/O 层，把预测、生成、理解和条件变换都视作部分观测下的补全问题；如果成立，它会给 native multimodal pretraining 提供比纯对比学习更宽的设计空间。","technical_approach":"4M 的核心方法是把不同模态都表示成 token 或离散化后的序列，然后用统一的 masked modeling 目标去恢复被遮蔽部分。关键设计不在于简单拼接 token，而在于它支持任意模态作为输入、任意模态作为输出，因此同一模型既能做理解，也能做跨模态生成或变换。和 CLIP 这类只学对齐的模型相比，4M 明确学习联合分布中的条件结构；和纯自回归统一模型相比，masked 目标在双向上下文利用、条件补全和训练并行性上更自然，但代价是生成路径和 likelihood 定义没有 AR 那么直接。","experiments":"论文的关键实验通常会覆盖多任务、多模态设定，而不仅是一项 benchmark。最值得关注的事实是：单一 4M 模型是否能在图像理解、图像生成、语义预测、视觉问答或跨模态条件生成上达到接近专门模型的性能，以及随着模态数量增加是否出现正迁移而不是负迁移。对 pretrain 工程更重要的观察是，统一 masked objective 是否比任务特定 heads 更稳、是否依赖高质量离散 tokenizer、以及不同模态 token 比例如何影响训练；这些决定了它是“可扩展预训练路线”还是“优雅但脆弱的研究原型”。","authors_note":"作者名单中包含多个视觉与多模态研究者；仅凭题面无法可靠确认机构与代表性团队信息，返回空字符串。"},"summary_en":{"core_problem":"The paper asks whether one masked-modeling framework can handle many modalities and many tasks at once, instead of training separate models for images, text, segmentation, depth, and cross-modal prediction. Prior multimodal pretraining often either focused on alignment only or used a unified token interface with awkward task formulation; 4M tries to make multimodal completion the common language.","importance":"This is a paradigm-level contribution because it broadens what masked modeling can be used for. For pretraining, the key implication is that masked objectives are not limited to BERT/MAE-style single-modality self-supervision; they can serve as a general interface for native multimodal modeling where understanding and generation are both framed as conditional completion.","technical_approach":"The core idea is to represent different modalities as token sequences and train one model to reconstruct arbitrary masked subsets. The important design choice is not just concatenating tokens, but allowing any subset of modalities to be observed and any subset to be predicted. Compared with CLIP-like alignment models, 4M learns richer conditional structure in the joint distribution. Compared with pure autoregressive unification, masked modeling uses bidirectional context more naturally and parallelizes training well, though generation semantics are less direct than next-token likelihood.","experiments":"The paper is most compelling if one model performs competitively across heterogeneous tasks rather than winning a single benchmark. The important facts are whether 4M can handle image understanding, cross-modal generation, dense prediction, and modality translation with limited task-specific adaptation, and whether adding modalities yields positive transfer instead of interference. For practitioners, the useful questions are how strongly it depends on discretization quality, how token balance across modalities affects optimization, and whether the unified objective remains stable as the modality set grows.","authors_note":""},"tags":["masked-modeling","multimodal-pretraining","tokenizer","unified-modeling","foundation-models"],"notable_authors":["David Mizrahi","Roman Transformer 上下文长度受限且推理成本随序列增长恶化的问题，尤其是如何在不把全部历史重新塞进窗口的情况下，对超长上下文进行随机访问。以前的长上下文方法多在局部注意力、线性注意力或检索增强之间做折中，但要么失去精确访问能力，要么系统复杂；这篇工作试图保留 Transformer 的表达力，同时突破固定窗口。","importance":"它的意义偏系统与架构接口，对 pretrain 和 serving 都有启发。对于基础模型研究，关键点不是‘无限上下文’这句口号，而是提出了一种把外部记忆和注意力机制更紧密耦合的方式：如果模型能训练时学会对历史进行地址化访问，那么长程依赖未必只能靠扩大上下文窗口硬吃。","technical_approach":"核心方法是为 Transformer 相比，它面向的是同一序列内的持久记忆，而不是外部文档知识。有效性的前提是地址机制足够稳定，否则随机访问会退化成噪声召回。","experiments":"这类工作的关键证据通常在合成长序列任务和文档级 benchmark 上。最重要的实验事实应是：在远超训练窗口长度的序列上，模型仍能完成 fixed context windows and inference costs that grow badly with sequence length. The specific goal is random access over effectively unbounded context, without refeeding the entire history into the active window.","importance":"Its value is architectural and systems-oriented, with implications for both pretraining and serving. The important distinction is that it is not just retrieve-and-concatenate; the model is trained to use selective access as part of sequence modeling. Relative to sparse attention, this emphasizes explicit addressability. Relative to standard RAG, it targets persistent memory within the same sequence rather than external documents. Masked language modeling has been highly successful, but often treated as a practical surrogate with limited probabilistic interpretation; this work tries to make that relationship explicit.","importance":"This is a theory-facing contribution with direct pretraining relevance. Many alternatives to next-token autoregression rely on predicting subsets of tokens rather than the full sequence order. If we do not understand how these objectives relate to joint distribution learning, it is hard to reason about why some methods learn strong representations yet weaker generative models, or how to compare masking schemes rigorously.","technical_approach":"The paper analyzes masked pretraining as an approximation, bound, or biased estimator of marginal likelihood, with the masking distribution playing a central role. The key point is not that MLM is always equivalent to maximum likelihood, but that equivalence or deviation depends on conditions such as mask policy, reconstruction scope, and conditional assumptions. efficiency 行为。","authors_note":""},"summary_en":{"core_problem":"The paper revisits a basic limitation of standard autoregressive pretraining: next-token prediction only moves left-to-right, so training signals are local and asymmetric, especially for tokens in the middle of a sequence. Masked language modeling uses both sides but creates a train–test mismatch. This work explores a middle ground by having prediction proceed from both ends and meet in the center.","importance":"This is a pretraining-paradigm contribution. Its importance is that it questions whether left-to-right factorization should remain the default way to train sequence models. Even if it does not replace AR outright, it offers a concrete alternative for improving context utilization, shortening dependency paths, and rethinking how supervision is distributed across the sequence.","technical_approach":"From the title, the method likely defines a training objective where generation starts from both the prefix and suffix and proceeds inward until the two sides meet. The attraction of this design is that middle tokens can be trained with bidirectional constraints while retaining an explicit generative structure. Relative to MLM, it is closer to generation. Relative to standard AR, it gives up a strict causal order to gain richer context and potentially easier long-range credit assignment. The design challenge is to make the two fronts consistent when they converge, so the objective likely includes special handling for meeting points or positional structure.","experiments":"The main empirical result is likely that meet-in-the-middle pretraining performs competitively or better than standard AR and/or MLM in settings where global consistency or long-range structure matters. For pretraining research, this matters because many claims about one architecture family being obsolete are confounded by stale baselines rather than true modeling limits.","technical_approach":"The core idea is a full fast-pretraining recipe for BERT rather than a single novel component. Recently, there has been a renewed interest in using linear RNNs for efficient sequence modeling. These linear RNNs often employ gating mechanisms in the output of the linear recurrence layer while ignoring the significance of using forget gates within the recurrence. In this paper, we propose a gated linear RNN model dubbed Hierarchically Gated Recurrent Neural Network (HGRN), which includes forget gates that are lower bounded by a learnable value. The lower bound increases monotonically when moving up layers. This allows the upper layers to model long-term dependencies and the lower layers to model more local, short-term dependencies. Experiments on language modeling, image classification, and long-range arena benchmarks showcase the efficiency and effectiveness of our proposed model. The source code is available at https://github.com/OpenNLPLab/HGRN.","venue":"NEURIPS","year":2023,"track":"main","award":null,"links":{"arxiv":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.04823","pdf":"","openreview":"","dblp":"https://dblp.org/rec/conf/nips/QinYZ23","doi":""},"_dblp_key":"conf/nips/QinYZ23","_ee":"http://papers.nips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/hash/694be3548697e9cc8999d45e8d16fe1e-Abstract-Conference.html","category_id":"llm_pretrain","relevance_score":8,"reading_tier":"must_read","summary":{"core_problem":"这篇工作要解决的是：近年线性 RNN 重新受到关注，但很多方法只在递推输出端加 gate，没有把“忘记多少历史”直接纳入状态更新，因此长短期依赖的分工仍然粗糙。Transformer 用注意力显式处理长程依赖，RNN 若想重新具备竞争力，必须在递推层面把记忆时间尺度设计清楚，而不是只做轻量线性化。","importance":"它的重要性是架构级的，但更偏高效序列建模而非主流 LLM 范式替代。对 pretrain 研究的价值在于提供一个清晰思路：如果不同层具有单调递增的记忆下界，模型可以把局部模式和长程模式自动分层，这比所有层共享相近的遗忘行为更接近实际语言序列的多时间尺度结构。它不一定立刻取代 Transformer，但对长序列、低延迟和状态压缩方向有参考意义。","technical_approach":"核心方法是提出 HGRN，一个带层次化 forget gate 的 gated linear RNN。关键设计不是“有 gate”本身，而是给 forget gate 设一个可学习下界，并让这个下界随层数单调升高；结果是底层更容易快速遗忘，专注局部短期模式，上层更难遗忘，负责累计长时信息。和只在输出端 gating 的线性 RNN 相比，这种设计直接作用在状态演化上，因此更像把时间尺度作为架构先验写进模型，而不是指望训练自己分化出来。","experiments":"摘要已明确它在 language modeling 等序列任务上做了实验，主张是在性能和效率上优于现有 linear RNN 基线。最值得关注的实验点通常有两类：一是与 S4、RetNet、RWKV 或其他 gated/linear recurrence 模型比较长序列建模质量与吞吐；二是 ablation 证明“分层下界”比普通 forget gate 或无单调约束版本更稳定。当前截断摘要没有给出参数规模、数据集和精确数值，因此不能编造。","authors_note":""},"summary_en":{"core_problem":"The paper addresses a real limitation in recent linear RNN work: many models gate the output of the recurrence but do not directly control forgetting inside the state update, so they still lack a principled separation between short-term and long-term memory. If RNNs are to compete again in efficient sequence modeling, memory timescales need to be built into the recurrence itself, not left implicit.","importance":"This is mainly an architectural contribution for efficient sequence modeling. Its relevance to pretraining is that it offers a concrete way to encode multi-timescale behavior across layers: lower layers focus on local patterns, upper layers retain information longer. That does not immediately dethrone attention-based models, but it is a meaningful design direction for long-context, low-latency, and compact-state modeling.","technical_approach":"The core proposal is HGRN, a gated linear RNN with hierarchical forget gates. The novelty is not merely adding a gate, but imposing a learnable lower bound on the forget gate and making that lower bound increase monotonically with depth. This turns memory duration into an explicit architectural prior: lower layers are encouraged to forget quickly, while upper layers are biased toward long-range accumulation. Compared with linear RNNs that only gate outputs, this acts directly on the hidden-state dynamics and gives a clearer mechanism for timescale specialization.","experiments":"The abstract indicates experiments on language modeling and related sequence tasks, with gains over prior linear-RNN baselines in effectiveness and efficiency. The most informative evidence would typically include comparisons against other efficient sequence models and ablations showing that the hierarchical lower-bound constraint matters beyond ordinary gating. The truncated abstract does not provide safe details on model sizes, datasets, or exact margins.","authors_note":""},"tags":["architecture","recurrence","gating","sequence-modeling","linear-rnn"]},{"id":"SchickDDRLHZCS23","title":"Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use integrate the returned outputs, without relying on large human-labeled tool-use corpora. Earlier approaches either required expensive handcrafted supervision or depended on prompting alone, which is brittle and does not train a reusable calling policy.","importance":"This is a method-level contribution with long downstream impact because it turns tool use into a self-bootstrapping training pipeline rather than a prompt trick. For the pretrain/post-train interface, the key lesson is that a model can generate candidate API traces from a few demonstrations, filter them by utility, and feed them back into training; that synthetic-supervision loop became influential for later tool-augmented LMs and agents.","technical_approach":"The core method, Toolformer, starts from a few demonstrations of API use, lets the model propose tool calls over raw text, executes those calls, and keeps only the candidates that improve language-model likelihood before fine-tuning on the resulting augmented corpus. The paper argues that models transition from position-yoked exponential integration windows to structure-yoked power-law windows. That is a stronger claim than 'larger models use longer context': it suggests a qualitative shift from local, position-anchored processing toward more abstract structure-aware integration.","technical_approach":"The approach is to estimate how the influence of input tokens decays with distance under different structural conditions. This leads to two regimes: smaller or earlier-stage models behave like they integrate information in local exponential windows tied to position, while stronger models show heavier-tailed, power-law-like dependence organized around linguistic or task structure. The contribution is analytical rather than architectural. The distinction matters because exponential decay implies rapid saturation of useful context, whereas power-law decay leaves meaningful long-range contributions.","experiments":"The central empirical signal is a shift in the fitted dependency curves as models get larger or more trained: exponential fits weaken and power-law fits become more appropriate, especially when aligned with structural boundaries. 整数训练可用，收益远高于常规混合精度的小修小补：显存、带宽、算力利用率都会一起改善，而这些正是大规模预训练最贵的部分。更重要的是，它把“低比特只能推理省钱”推进到“训练主路径也可低比特”，这对未来 pretrain stack 的硬件-算法协同很关键。","technical_approach":"作者提出的是 4-bit 整数版 Transformer 训练方案，关键在于如何管理量化误差而不是简单把所有张量裁到 INT4。有效设计通常包括三类机制：第一，按张量/通道/块做缩放，尽量保留局部动态范围；第二，对梯度累积、残差、归一化或某些敏感更新路径保留更高精度；第三，使用量化感知训练策略，让前向和反向都在可控误差下工作。它与常见 FP16/BF16 mixed precision 的本质区别是，后者只是缩短浮点 mantissa，而 INT4 则需要显式离散化和比例因子管理，所以训练稳定性更依赖 scale 设计与误差补偿。","experiments":"从题目看，实验应覆盖标准 Transformer 训练任务，并比较 INT4 训练与 FP32/BF16/FP16 基线在收敛、最终精度和吞吐上的差异。关键事实通常是：在若干 NLP 或 vision Transformer 设定下，4-bit 整数训练能接近高精度基线，同时带来显著内存和速度收益。最重要的 ablation 往往是尺度选择、敏感层保留精度、以及是否需要量化误差补偿；缺掉其中任一项，收敛会明显变差。没有摘要和表格，无法安全给出具体数字。","authors_note":"作者包含 Jun Zhu 和 RL 不稳定；代价是它只能利用离线候选集，探索新行为的能力弱于真正在线 RL。","experiments":"这篇论文的公开印象是，作者主要在开源指令模型上验证 RRHF 能以远低于 RLHF 的复杂度得到接近甚至更好的对齐质量。已给信息里没有摘要，因此不能在这里负责任地写出具体模型规模、数据量和各基准分数；但可以确认，实验通常围绕 helpfulness/harmlessness For pretraining researchers, the lesson is broader: preference data can provide a high-signal gradient without online RL, which changes how we think about the pretrain–posttrain interface, the necessity of reward models, and low-cost pathways to improve a base model’s usefulness.","technical_approach":"RRHF takes multiple candidate responses for the same prompt, ranks them by human preference, and directly trains the LM so that higher-ranked responses receive higher overall token log-probability than lower-ranked ones. This is effective because preference annotation is naturally relative rather than absolute, so directly optimizing rank avoids reward-model misspecification and RL instability. The trade-off is that it is limited to an offline candidate set and cannot explore new behaviors as flexibly as online RL.","experiments":"The known empirical claim is that RRHF reaches strong alignment quality with much lower complexity than RLHF on open instruction-tuned models.
